infobox.value: Nablus and [Mount Gerizim](/wiki/Mount_Gerizim "Mount Gerizim") skyline; [Manara Clock Tower](/wiki/Manara_Clock_Tower "Manara Clock Tower") and [An-Nasr Mosque](/wiki/An-Nasr_Mosque "An-Nasr Mosque"); [Joseph's Tomb](/wiki/Joseph%27s_Tomb "Joseph's Tomb") chamber; Old City of Nablus; [Tell Balata](/wiki/Tell_Balata "Tell Balata") archaeological site; Eastern Orthodox Church of Bir Ya'qub, where [Jacob's Well](/wiki/Jacob%27s_Well "Jacob's Well") is located; [Mount Ebal](/wiki/Mount_Ebal "Mount Ebal"); and a Nablus shopping district. | |

([/ˈnæbləs, ˈnɑːbləs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*NA(H)B-ləs*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[a]](#cite_note-modern_langs-5)[[b]](#cite_note-dead_langs-6) is a city in the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank"), [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine "Palestine"), and the capital of the [Nablus Governorate](/wiki/Nablus_Governorate "Nablus Governorate"). It is located approximately 49 kilometres (30 mi) north of [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"),[[5]](#cite_note-7) between [Mount Ebal](/wiki/Mount_Ebal "Mount Ebal") and [Mount Gerizim](/wiki/Mount_Gerizim "Mount Gerizim") with a population of 156,906.[[1]](#cite_note-PrelimCensus2017-1) The city is a commercial and cultural centre of [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine "Palestine"), home to [An-Najah National University](/wiki/An-Najah_National_University "An-Najah National University"), one of the largest institutions of higher learning in Palestine, and the [Palestine Stock Exchange](/wiki/Palestine_Exchange "Palestine Exchange").[[6]](#cite_note-Bishara-8) Nablus is under the administration of the [Palestinian National Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_National_Authority "Palestinian National Authority") (PNA).

**Nablus** ([/ˈnæbləs, ˈnɑːbləs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*NA(H)B-ləs*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[a]](#cite_note-modern_langs-5)[[b]](#cite_note-dead_langs-6) is a city in the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank"), [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine "Palestine"), and the capital of the [Nablus Governorate](/wiki/Nablus_Governorate "Nablus Governorate"). It is located approximately 49 kilometres (30 mi) north of [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"),[[5]](#cite_note-7) between [Mount Ebal](/wiki/Mount_Ebal "Mount Ebal") and [Mount Gerizim](/wiki/Mount_Gerizim "Mount Gerizim") with a population of 156,906.[[1]](#cite_note-PrelimCensus2017-1) The city is a commercial and cultural centre of [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine "Palestine"), home to [An-Najah National University](/wiki/An-Najah_National_University "An-Najah National University"), one of the largest institutions of higher learning in Palestine, and the [Palestine Stock Exchange](/wiki/Palestine_Exchange "Palestine Exchange").[[6]](#cite_note-Bishara-8) Nablus is under the administration of the [Palestinian National Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_National_Authority "Palestinian National Authority") (PNA).

Nablus has been traditionally associated with the ancient city of [Shechem](/wiki/Shechem "Shechem").[[7]](#cite_note-9) However, Shechem is now identified with the nearby site of [Tell Balata](/wiki/Tell_Balata "Tell Balata") in the [Balata al-Balad](/wiki/Balata_al-Balad "Balata al-Balad") suburb of the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank").
The modern name of the city can be traced back to the [Roman period](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), when it was named *Flavia Neapolis* by Roman emperor [Vespasian](/wiki/Vespasian "Vespasian") in 72 CE. During the [Byzantine period](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), conflict between the city's [Samaritan](/wiki/Samaritans "Samaritans") and newer [Christian](/wiki/Christians "Christians") inhabitants peaked in the [Samaritan revolts](/wiki/Samaritan_revolts "Samaritan revolts") that were eventually suppressed by the Byzantines by 573, which greatly dwindled the Samaritan population of the city. Following the [Muslim conquest of the Levant](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Levant "Muslim conquest of the Levant") in the 7th century, the city was given its present-day Arabic name of *Nablus*. After the [First Crusade](/wiki/First_Crusade "First Crusade"), the [Crusaders](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades") drafted the laws of the [Kingdom of Jerusalem](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Jerusalem "Kingdom of Jerusalem") in the [Council of Nablus](/wiki/Council_of_Nablus "Council of Nablus"), and its Christian, Samaritan, and [Muslim](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") inhabitants prospered. The city then came under the control of the [Ayyubids](/wiki/Ayyubid_dynasty "Ayyubid dynasty") and the [Mamluk Sultanate](/wiki/Mamluk_Sultanate "Mamluk Sultanate"). Under the [Ottoman Turks](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), who [conquered the city in 1517](/wiki/Ottoman%E2%80%93Mamluk_War_(1516%E2%80%931517) "Ottoman–Mamluk War (1516–1517)"), Nablus served as the administrative and commercial centre for the surrounding area corresponding to the modern-day northern West Bank. Much of Nablus' history is preserved in [its Old City](/wiki/Old_City_of_Nablus "Old City of Nablus"), which contains more than 100 monumental buildings.

After the city was captured by [British](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") forces during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), Nablus was incorporated into [Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine") in 1922. The [1948 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War") saw the entire West Bank, including Nablus, [occupied and annexed](/wiki/Jordanian_annexation_of_the_West_Bank "Jordanian annexation of the West Bank") by [Transjordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"). Since the [1967 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War"), the West Bank [has been occupied](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_West_Bank "Israeli occupation of the West Bank") by [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"); since 1995, it has been governed by the [Palestinian Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_Authority "Palestinian Authority") as part of [Area A of the West Bank](/wiki/Palestinian_enclaves "Palestinian enclaves"). Today, the population is predominantly Muslim, with small Christian and Samaritan minorities.

## History

### Classical antiquity

Further information: [Samaritan Revolts](/wiki/Samaritan_Revolts "Samaritan Revolts") and [Samaria](/wiki/Samaria "Samaria")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Istanbul_Archaeology_Museum_Statue_of_Silenus%2C_1st_C_CE_Neapolis_%28Palestina%29_4368.jpg/250px-Istanbul_Archaeology_Museum_Statue_of_Silenus%2C_1st_C_CE_Neapolis_%28Palestina%29_4368.jpg)](/wiki/File:Istanbul_Archaeology_Museum_Statue_of_Silenus,_1st_C_CE_Neapolis_(Palestina)_4368.jpg)

A 1st century CE statue of [Silenus](/wiki/Silenus "Silenus") discovered in Nablus

*Flavia Neapolis* ('new city of the emperor [Flavius](/wiki/Flavius "Flavius")') was named in 72 CE by the [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") emperor [Vespasian](/wiki/Vespasian "Vespasian") and applied to an older [Samaritan](/wiki/Samaritan "Samaritan") village, variously called *Mabartha* ('the passage')[[8]](#cite_note-Negevp175-10) or *Mamorpha*.[[9]](#cite_note-11) Located between [Mount Ebal](/wiki/Mount_Ebal "Mount Ebal") and [Mount Gerizim](/wiki/Mount_Gerizim "Mount Gerizim"), the new city lay 2 kilometers (1.2 mi) west of the [Biblical](/wiki/Bible "Bible") city of [Shechem](/wiki/Shechem "Shechem") which was destroyed by the Romans that same year during the [First Jewish–Roman War](/wiki/First_Jewish%E2%80%93Roman_War "First Jewish–Roman War").[[10]](#cite_note-STF-12)[[11]](#cite_note-DNTA-13) Holy places at the site of the city's founding include [Joseph's Tomb](/wiki/Joseph%27s_Tomb "Joseph's Tomb") and [Jacob's Well](/wiki/Jacob%27s_Well "Jacob's Well"). Because of the city's strategic geographic position and the abundance of water from nearby springs, Neapolis prospered, accumulating extensive territory, including the former [Judean](/wiki/Judea "Judea") [toparchy](/wiki/Toparchy "Toparchy") of [Acraba](/wiki/Aqraba,_Nablus "Aqraba, Nablus").[[10]](#cite_note-STF-12)

Insofar as the hilly topography of the site would allow, the city was built on a Roman [grid plan](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan") and settled with veterans who fought in the victorious legions and other foreign colonists.[[8]](#cite_note-Negevp175-10) In the 2nd century CE, Emperor [Hadrian](/wiki/Hadrian "Hadrian") built a grand [theater](/wiki/Roman_theatre_(structure) "Roman theatre (structure)") in Neapolis that could seat up to 7,000 people.[[12]](#cite_note-Semplici-14) Coins found in Nablus dating to this period depict Roman military emblems and gods and goddesses of the Greek pantheon such as [Zeus](/wiki/Zeus "Zeus"), [Artemis](/wiki/Artemis "Artemis"), [Serapis](/wiki/Serapis "Serapis"), and [Asklepios](/wiki/Asklepios "Asklepios").[[8]](#cite_note-Negevp175-10) Neapolis was entirely [pagan](/wiki/Paganism "Paganism") at this time.[[8]](#cite_note-Negevp175-10) [Justin Martyr](/wiki/Justin_Martyr "Justin Martyr") who was born in the city c. 100 CE, came into contact with [Platonism](/wiki/Platonism "Platonism"), but not with Christians there.[[8]](#cite_note-Negevp175-10) The city flourished until the civil war between [Septimius Severus](/wiki/Septimius_Severus "Septimius Severus") and [Pescennius Niger](/wiki/Pescennius_Niger "Pescennius Niger") in 198–9 CE. Having sided with Niger, who was defeated, the city was temporarily stripped of its legal privileges by Severus, who designated these to [Sebastia](/wiki/Sebastia,_Nablus "Sebastia, Nablus") instead.[[8]](#cite_note-Negevp175-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Volusian_Neapolis.jpg/250px-Volusian_Neapolis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Volusian_Neapolis.jpg)

Coin minted in Nablus (*Neapolis*), in the name of Emperor [Volusian](/wiki/Volusian "Volusian"), 251–253 CE

In 244 CE, [Philip the Arab](/wiki/Philip_the_Arab "Philip the Arab") transformed Flavius Neapolis into a Roman colony named *Julia Neapolis*. It retained this status until the rule of [Trebonianus Gallus](/wiki/Trebonianus_Gallus "Trebonianus Gallus") in 251 CE. The *[Encyclopaedia Judaica](/wiki/Encyclopaedia_Judaica "Encyclopaedia Judaica")* speculates that Christianity was dominant in the 2nd or 3rd century, with some sources positing a later date of 480 CE.[[13]](#cite_note-OCHA-15) It is known for certain that a bishop from Nablus participated in the [Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea") in 325 CE.[[14]](#cite_note-Negevp176-16) The presence of Samaritans in the city is attested to in literary and epigraphic evidence dating to the 4th century CE.[[14]](#cite_note-Negevp176-16) As yet, there is no evidence attesting to a Jewish presence in ancient Neapolis.[[14]](#cite_note-Negevp176-16)

Si'on suggested that Neapolis was about 900 acres (360 ha) in size during the [Byzantine period](/wiki/Byzantine_Palestine "Byzantine Palestine"), making it three times larger than it was when it was first established as a Roman colony.[[15]](#cite_note-Si2001-17) Magen estimates that around 20,000 people lived there during this period.[[16]](#cite_note-Magen2005-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Ancient_ruins_in_a_Nablus_neighborhood.JPG/250px-Ancient_ruins_in_a_Nablus_neighborhood.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ancient_ruins_in_a_Nablus_neighborhood.JPG)

Ruins from antiquity (foreground) in a residential area in Nablus, 2008

Conflict among the Christian population of Neapolis emerged in 451. By this time, Neapolis was within the [Palaestina Prima](/wiki/Palaestina_Prima "Palaestina Prima") province under the rule of the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"). The tension was a result of [Monophysite](/wiki/Monophysitism "Monophysitism") Christian attempts to prevent the return of the [Patriarch of Jerusalem](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Patriarch_of_Jerusalem "Greek Orthodox Patriarch of Jerusalem"), [Juvenal](/wiki/Juvenal_of_Jerusalem "Juvenal of Jerusalem"), to his [episcopal see](/wiki/Episcopal_see "Episcopal see").[[10]](#cite_note-STF-12) However, the conflict did not grow into civil strife.

As tensions among the Christians of Neapolis decreased, tensions between the Christian community and the [Samaritans](/wiki/Samaritans "Samaritans") grew dramatically. In 484, the city became the site of a deadly encounter between the two groups, provoked by rumors that the Christians intended to transfer the remains of [Aaron](/wiki/Aaron "Aaron")'s sons and grandsons [Eleazar](/wiki/Eleazar "Eleazar"), [Ithamar](/wiki/Ithamar "Ithamar") and [Phinehas](/wiki/Phinehas "Phinehas"). Samaritans reacted by entering the cathedral of Neapolis, killing the Christians inside and severing the fingers of the bishop Terebinthus. Terebinthus then fled to [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople"), requesting an army garrison to prevent further attacks. As a result of the revolt, the Byzantine emperor [Zeno](/wiki/Zeno_(emperor) "Zeno (emperor)") erected a church dedicated to [Mary](/wiki/Mary_(mother_of_Jesus) "Mary (mother of Jesus)") on Mount Gerizim. He also forbade the Samaritans to travel to the mountain to celebrate their religious ceremonies, and expropriated their synagogue there. These actions by the emperor fueled Samaritan anger towards the Christians further.[[10]](#cite_note-STF-12)

Thus, the Samaritans rebelled again under the rule of emperor [Anastasius I](/wiki/Anastasius_I_(emperor) "Anastasius I (emperor)"), reoccupying Mount Gerizim, which was subsequently reconquered by the Byzantine governor of [Edessa](/wiki/Edessa,_Mesopotamia "Edessa, Mesopotamia"), Procopius. A third Samaritan revolt which took place under the leadership of [Julianus ben Sabar](/wiki/Julianus_ben_Sabar "Julianus ben Sabar") in 529 was perhaps the most violent. Neapolis' bishop [Ammonas](/wiki/Ammonas "Ammonas") was murdered and the city's priests were hacked into pieces and then burned together with the relics of [saints](/wiki/Saint "Saint"). The forces of Emperor [Justinian I](/wiki/Justinian_I "Justinian I") were sent in to quell the revolt, which ended with the slaughter of the majority of the Samaritan population in the city.[[10]](#cite_note-STF-12)

### Early Muslim period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Umm_ar-Rasas_Church_of_St._Stephen_Nablus_2786.jpg/250px-Umm_ar-Rasas_Church_of_St._Stephen_Nablus_2786.jpg)](/wiki/File:Umm_ar-Rasas_Church_of_St._Stephen_Nablus_2786.jpg)

Depiction of Nablus (Neapolis) in the [Umm ar-Rasas mosaics](/wiki/Umm_ar-Rasas_mosaics "Umm ar-Rasas mosaics"), 8th century CE

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Nablus_Great_Mosque-cropped.JPG/250px-Nablus_Great_Mosque-cropped.JPG)](/wiki/File:Nablus_Great_Mosque-cropped.JPG)

Minaret and entrance of 10th-century [Great Mosque of Nablus](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Nablus "Great Mosque of Nablus"), 1908

Neapolis, along with most of Palestine, [was conquered by the Muslims](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Levant "Muslim conquest of the Levant") under [Khalid ibn al-Walid](/wiki/Khalid_ibn_al-Walid "Khalid ibn al-Walid"), a general of the [Rashidun army](/wiki/Rashidun_army "Rashidun army") of [Umar ibn al-Khattab](/wiki/Umar "Umar"), in 636 after the [Battle of Yarmouk](/wiki/Battle_of_Yarmouk "Battle of Yarmouk").[[10]](#cite_note-STF-12)[[11]](#cite_note-DNTA-13) The city's name was retained in its [Arabicized](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") form, *Nabulus*. The town prevailed as an important trade center during the centuries of [Islamic](/wiki/Islam "Islam") [Arab](/wiki/Arab "Arab") rule under the [Umayyad](/wiki/Umayyad "Umayyad"), [Abbasid](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate") and [Fatimid](/wiki/Fatimid "Fatimid") dynasties.

Under Muslim rule, Nablus contained a diverse population of Arabs and [Persians](/wiki/Persian_people "Persian people"), Muslims, Samaritans, Christians and [Jews](/wiki/Jew "Jew").[[10]](#cite_note-STF-12) In the 9th century CE, [Al-Yaqubi](/wiki/Ya%27qubi "Ya'qubi") reported that Nablus had a mixed population of Arabs, [Ajam](/wiki/Ajam "Ajam") (Non-Arabs), and Samaritans.[[17]](#cite_note-19) In the 10th century, the Arab geographer [al-Muqaddasi](/wiki/Al-Muqaddasi "Al-Muqaddasi"), described it as abundant of olive trees, with a large marketplace, a finely paved [Great Mosque](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Nablus "Great Mosque of Nablus"), houses built of stone, a stream running through the center of the city, and notable mills.[[18]](#cite_note-Muqaddasip55-20) He also noted that it was nicknamed "Little [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus")."[[12]](#cite_note-Semplici-14)[[18]](#cite_note-Muqaddasip55-20) At the time, the linen produced in Nablus was well known throughout the [Old World](/wiki/Old_World "Old World").[[19]](#cite_note-Runcimanp353-21)

### Crusader period

See also: [Vassals of the Kingdom of Jerusalem](/wiki/Vassals_of_the_Kingdom_of_Jerusalem "Vassals of the Kingdom of Jerusalem")

The city was captured by [Crusaders](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades") in 1099, under the command of [Prince Tancred](/wiki/Tancred,_Prince_of_Galilee "Tancred, Prince of Galilee"), and renamed *Naples*.[[10]](#cite_note-STF-12) Though the Crusaders extorted many supplies from the population for their troops who were en route to Jerusalem, they did not sack the city, presumably because of the large Christian population there.[[20]](#cite_note-Andersonp72-22) Nablus became part of the [royal domain](/wiki/Royal_domain "Royal domain") of the [Kingdom of Jerusalem](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Jerusalem "Kingdom of Jerusalem"). The Muslim, Eastern Orthodox Christian, and Samaritan populations remained in the city and were joined by some Crusaders who settled therein to take advantage of the city's abundant resources. In 1120, the Crusaders convened the [Council of Nablus](/wiki/Council_of_Nablus "Council of Nablus") out of which was issued the first written laws for the kingdom.[[10]](#cite_note-STF-12) They converted the Samaritan synagogue in Nablus into a church.[[20]](#cite_note-Andersonp72-22) The Samaritan community built a new synagogue in the 1130s.[[21]](#cite_note-Smithp70-23) In 1137, Arab and [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_people "Turkish people") troops stationed in [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus") raided Nablus, killing many Christians and burning down the city's churches. However, they were unsuccessful in retaking the city.[[10]](#cite_note-STF-12) Queen [Melisende of Jerusalem](/wiki/Melisende_of_Jerusalem "Melisende of Jerusalem") resided in Nablus from 1150 to 1161, after she was granted control over the city in order to resolve a dispute with her son [Baldwin III](/wiki/Baldwin_III_of_Jerusalem "Baldwin III of Jerusalem"). Crusaders began building Christian institutions in Nablus, including a church dedicated to the [Passion](/wiki/Passion_(Christianity) "Passion (Christianity)") and Resurrection of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus"), and in 1170 they erected a hospice for pilgrims.[[10]](#cite_note-STF-12)

### Ayyubid and Mamluk rule

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Interior_view_of_Nasr_Mosque.JPG/250px-Interior_view_of_Nasr_Mosque.JPG)](/wiki/File:Interior_view_of_Nasr_Mosque.JPG)

Interior view of the [An-Nasr Mosque](/wiki/An-Nasr_Mosque "An-Nasr Mosque"), converted from a [Crusader](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades") church to a mosque in the 13th century

Crusader rule came to an end in 1187, when the [Ayyubids](/wiki/Ayyubid "Ayyubid") led by [Saladin](/wiki/Saladin "Saladin") captured the city. According to a liturgical manuscript in [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language"), [Latin Christians](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") fled Nablus, but the original [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") Christian inhabitants remained.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Syrian geographer [Yaqut al-Hamawi](/wiki/Yaqut_al-Hamawi "Yaqut al-Hamawi") (1179–1229), wrote that Ayyubid Nablus was a "celebrated city in Filastin (Palestine)... having wide lands and a fine district." He also mentions the large Samaritan population in the city.[[22]](#cite_note-le_Strange-24) After its recapture by the Muslims, the [Great Mosque of Nablus](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Nablus "Great Mosque of Nablus"), which had become a church under Crusader rule, was restored as a mosque by the Ayyubids, who also built a [mausoleum](/wiki/Mausoleum "Mausoleum") in the old city.[[13]](#cite_note-OCHA-15)

In October 1242, Nablus was raided by the [Knights Templar](/wiki/Knights_Templar "Knights Templar"). This was the conclusion of the 1242 campaign season in which the Templars had joined forces with the Ayyubid emir of Kerak, [An-Nasir Dawud](/wiki/An-Nasir_Dawud "An-Nasir Dawud"), against the Mamluks. The Templars raided Nablus in revenge for a previous massacre of Christians by their erstwhile ally An-Nasir Dawud. The attack is reported as a particularly bloody affair lasting for three days, during which the Mosque was burned and many residents of the city, Christians alongside Muslims, were killed or sold in the slave markets of [Acre](/wiki/Acre,_Israel "Acre, Israel").
The successful raid was widely publicized by the Templars in Europe; it is thought to be depicted in a late 13th-century fresco in the Templar church of [San Bevignate](/wiki/San_Bevignate "San Bevignate"), [Perugia](/wiki/Perugia "Perugia").[[23]](#cite_note-25)

The Samaritan synagogue, built in 362 by the high priest Akbon, and later converted into a church by the Crusaders, was converted into [al-Khadra Mosque](/wiki/Al-Khadra_Mosque "Al-Khadra Mosque") in 1244. Two other Crusader churches became the [An-Nasr Mosque](/wiki/An-Nasr_Mosque "An-Nasr Mosque") and al-Masakim Mosque during that century.[[10]](#cite_note-STF-12)[[20]](#cite_note-Andersonp72-22)

The [Mamluk dynasty](/wiki/Mamluk_Sultanate_(Cairo) "Mamluk Sultanate (Cairo)") gained control of Nablus in 1260 and during their reign, they built numerous mosques and schools.[[11]](#cite_note-DNTA-13) Under Mamluk rule, Nablus possessed running water, many [Turkish baths](/wiki/Turkish_bath "Turkish bath") and exported olive oil and [soap](/wiki/Nabulsi_soap "Nabulsi soap") to [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), Syria, the [Hejaz](/wiki/Hejaz "Hejaz"), several [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean") islands, and the [Arabian Desert](/wiki/Arabian_Desert "Arabian Desert"). The city's olive oil was also used in the [Umayyad Mosque](/wiki/Umayyad_Mosque "Umayyad Mosque") in Damascus. [Ibn Battuta](/wiki/Ibn_Battuta "Ibn Battuta"), the Arab explorer, visited Nablus in 1355, and described it as a city "full of trees and streams and full of olives." He noted that the city grew and exported [carob](/wiki/Carob "Carob") jam to [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo") and Damascus.[[22]](#cite_note-le_Strange-24)

### Ottoman era

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Louis-Fran%C3%A7ois_Cassas%2C_Vue_Generale_de_Neapolis%2C_dite_vulgairement_Napelouse_et_rempla%C3%A7ant_l%27ancienne_Sichem.jpg/250px-Louis-Fran%C3%A7ois_Cassas%2C_Vue_Generale_de_Neapolis%2C_dite_vulgairement_Napelouse_et_rempla%C3%A7ant_l%27ancienne_Sichem.jpg)](/wiki/File:Louis-Fran%C3%A7ois_Cassas,_Vue_Generale_de_Neapolis,_dite_vulgairement_Napelouse_et_rempla%C3%A7ant_l%27ancienne_Sichem.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Nablous%2C_The_Ancient_Shechem_MET_DP116336.jpg/250px-Nablous%2C_The_Ancient_Shechem_MET_DP116336.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nablous,_The_Ancient_Shechem_MET_DP116336.jpg)

Nablus came under the rule of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") in 1517, along with the whole of Palestine. The Ottomans divided Palestine into six *[sanjaks](/wiki/Sanjak "Sanjak")* ('districts'): [Safad](/wiki/Safad "Safad"), [Jenin](/wiki/Jenin "Jenin"), [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"), [Gaza](/wiki/Gaza_City "Gaza City"), [Ajlun](/wiki/Ajlun "Ajlun") and [Nablus](/wiki/District_of_Nablus "District of Nablus"), all of which were part of [Ottoman Syria](/wiki/Ottoman_Syria "Ottoman Syria"). These five *sanjaks* were subdistricts of the [Vilayet of Damascus](/wiki/Vilayet_of_Damascus "Vilayet of Damascus"). Sanjak Nablus was further subdivided into five *[nahiya](/wiki/Nahiya "Nahiya")* ('subdistricts'), in addition to the city itself. The Ottomans did not attempt to restructure the political configuration of the region on the local level such that the borders of the *nahiya* were drawn to coincide with the historic strongholds of certain families. Nablus was only one among a number of local centers of power within Jabal Nablus, and its relations with the surrounding villages, such as [Beita](/wiki/Beita,_Nablus "Beita, Nablus") and [Aqraba](/wiki/Aqraba,_Nablus "Aqraba, Nablus"), were partially mediated by the rural-based chiefs of the *nahiya*.[[24]](#cite_note-Doumani3-26) During the 16th century, the population was predominantly Muslim, with Jewish, Samaritan and Christian minorities.[[10]](#cite_note-STF-12)[[25]](#cite_note-27)[[26]](#cite_note-Hutteroth-28)

After decades of upheavals and rebellions mounted by Arab tribes in the Middle East, the Ottomans attempted to reassert centralized control over the Arab *vilayets*. In 1657, they sent an expeditionary force led mostly by Arab *[sipahi](/wiki/Sipahi "Sipahi")* officers from central [Syria](/wiki/Ottoman_Syria "Ottoman Syria") to reassert Ottoman authority in Nablus and its hinterland, as part of a broader attempt to established centralized rule throughout the empire at that time. In return for their services, the officers were granted agricultural lands around the villages of Jabal Nablus. The Ottomans, fearing that the new Arab land holders would establish independent bases of power, dispersed the land plots to separate and distant locations within Jabal Nablus to avoid creating contiguous territory controlled by individual clans. Contrary to its centralization purpose, the 1657 campaign allowed the Arab *sipahi* officers to establish their own increasingly autonomous foothold in Nablus. The officers raised their families there and intermarried with the local notables of the area, namely the [ulama](/wiki/Ulama "Ulama") and merchant families. Without abandoning their nominal military service, they acquired diverse properties to consolidate their presence and income such as soap and pottery factories, [bathhouses](/wiki/Turkish_bath "Turkish bath"), agricultural lands, grain mills and, olive and sesame oil presses.[[24]](#cite_note-Doumani3-26)

The most influential military family were the Nimrs, who were originally local governors of [Homs](/wiki/Homs "Homs") and [Hama](/wiki/Hama "Hama")'s rural subdistricts. Other officer families included the Akhrami, Asqalan, Bayram, Jawhari, Khammash, Mir'i, Shafi, Sultan and Tamimi families, some of which remained in active service, while some left service for other pursuits. In the years following the 1657 campaign, two other families migrated to Nablus: the Jarrars from [Balqa](/wiki/Balqa_(region) "Balqa (region)") and the [Tuqans](/wiki/Tuqan_family "Tuqan family") from northern Syria or Transjordan. The Jarrars came to dominate the hinterland of Nablus, while the Tuqans and Nimrs competed for influence in the town. The former held the post of *[mutasallim](/wiki/Mutasallim "Mutasallim")* ('tax collector, strongman') of Nablus longer, though non-consecutively than any other family. The three families maintained their power until the mid-19th century.[[24]](#cite_note-Doumani3-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Tristram142a.jpg/250px-Tristram142a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tristram142a.jpg)

Nablus, by [W. C. P. Medlycott](/wiki/Medlycott_baronets "Medlycott baronets"), in [H. B. Tristram](/wiki/Henry_Baker_Tristram "Henry Baker Tristram"), 1865[[27]](#cite_note-29)

In the mid-18th century, [Daher al-Umar](/wiki/Daher_al-Umar "Daher al-Umar"), the autonomous Arab ruler of the [Galilee](/wiki/Galilee "Galilee") became a dominant figure in Palestine. To build up his army, he strove to gain a monopoly over the [cotton](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton") and olive oil trade of the [southern Levant](/wiki/Southern_Levant "Southern Levant"), including Jabal Nablus, which was a major producer of both crops. In 1771, during the [Egyptian Mamluk](/wiki/Ali_Bey_al-Kabir "Ali Bey al-Kabir") invasion of Syria, Daher aligned himself with the Mamluks and besieged Nablus, but did not succeed in taking the city. In 1773, he tried again without success. Nevertheless, from a political perspective, the sieges led to a decline in the importance of the city in favor of Acre. Daher's successor, [Jezzar Pasha](/wiki/Jezzar_Pasha "Jezzar Pasha"), maintained Acre's dominance over Nablus. After his reign ended in 1804, Nablus regained its autonomy, and the Tuqans, who represented a principal opposing force, rose to power.[[28]](#cite_note-Doumani4-30)

### Egyptian rule and Ottoman revival

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Nablus_1898.jpg/250px-Nablus_1898.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nablus_1898.jpg)

Nablus in 1898

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/F%C3%A9lix_Bonfils_Jeune_femme_de_Naplouse.jpg/250px-F%C3%A9lix_Bonfils_Jeune_femme_de_Naplouse.jpg)](/wiki/File:F%C3%A9lix_Bonfils_Jeune_femme_de_Naplouse.jpg)

Young woman from Nablus, between 1867 and 1885

In 1831–32 [Khedivate Egypt](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Dynasty "Muhammad Ali Dynasty"), then led by [Muhammad Ali](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_of_Egypt "Muhammad Ali of Egypt"), conquered Palestine from the Ottomans. A policy of [conscription](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription") and new [taxation](/wiki/Taxation "Taxation") was instituted which led to a [revolt](/wiki/Peasants%27_Revolt_of_1834_(Palestine) "Peasants' Revolt of 1834 (Palestine)") organized by the *[a'ayan](/wiki/Ayan_(class) "Ayan (class)")* (notables) of Nablus, [Hebron](/wiki/Hebron "Hebron") and the Jerusalem–Jaffa area. In May 1834, [Qasim al-Ahmad](/wiki/Qasim_al-Ahmad "Qasim al-Ahmad")—the chief of the [Jamma'in](/wiki/Jamma%27in "Jamma'in") *nahiya*—rallied the rural sheikhs and *[fellahin](/wiki/Fellahin "Fellahin")* (peasants) of Jabal Nablus and launched a revolt against Governor [Ibrahim Pasha](/wiki/Ibrahim_Pasha_of_Egypt "Ibrahim Pasha of Egypt"), in protest at conscription orders, among other new policies. The leaders of Nablus and its hinterland sent thousands of rebels to attack Jerusalem, the center of government authority in Palestine, aided by the [Abu Ghosh](/wiki/Abu_Ghosh "Abu Ghosh") clan, and they conquered the city on 31 May. However, they were later defeated by Ibrahim Pasha's forces the next month. Ibrahim then forced the heads of the Jabal Nablus clans to leave for nearby villages. By the end of August, the countrywide revolt had been suppressed and Qasim was executed.[[28]](#cite_note-Doumani4-30)

Egyptian rule in Palestine resulted in the destruction of [Acre](/wiki/Acre,_Israel "Acre, Israel") and thus, the political importance of Nablus was further elevated. The Ottomans wrested back control of Palestine from [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt_Eyalet "Egypt Eyalet") in 1840–41. However, the [Arraba](/wiki/Arraba,_Jenin "Arraba, Jenin")-based Abd al-Hadi clan which rose to prominence under Egyptian rule for supporting Ibrahim Pasha, continued its political dominance in Jabal Nablus.[[28]](#cite_note-Doumani4-30)

Throughout the 18th and 19th centuries, Nablus was the principal trade and manufacturing center in Ottoman Syria. Its economic activity and regional leadership position surpassed that of Jerusalem and the coastal cities of [Jaffa](/wiki/Jaffa "Jaffa") and Acre. [Olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil") was the primary product of Nablus and aided other related industries such as [soap-making](/wiki/Nabulsi_soap "Nabulsi soap") and basket weaving.[[29]](#cite_note-Doumani-31) It was also the largest producer of cotton in the Levant, topping the production of northern cities such as Damascus.[[30]](#cite_note-Doumani5-32) Jabal Nablus enjoyed a greater degree of [autonomy](/wiki/Autonomy "Autonomy") than other *sanjaks* under Ottoman control, probably because the city was the capital of a hilly region, in which there were no "foreigners" who held any military or bureaucratic posts. Thus, Nablus remained outside the direct "supervision" of the Ottoman government, according to historian [Beshara Doumani](/wiki/Beshara_Doumani "Beshara Doumani").[[29]](#cite_note-Doumani-31)

### World War I and British Mandate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Nablus_1918.jpg/250px-Nablus_1918.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nablus_1918.jpg)

Nablus in 1918

Between 19 September and 25 September 1918, in the last months of the [Sinai and Palestine Campaign](/wiki/Sinai_and_Palestine_Campaign "Sinai and Palestine Campaign") of the First World War the Battle of Nablus took place, together with the [Battle of Sharon](/wiki/Battle_of_Sharon "Battle of Sharon") during the set piece [Battle of Megiddo](/wiki/Battle_of_Megiddo_(1918) "Battle of Megiddo (1918)"). Fighting took place in the [Judean Hills](/wiki/Judean_Hills "Judean Hills") where the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire")'s [XX Corps](/wiki/XX_Corps_(United_Kingdom) "XX Corps (United Kingdom)") and [Royal Flying Corps](/wiki/Royal_Flying_Corps "Royal Flying Corps") attacked the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire")'s [Yildirim Army Group](/wiki/Yildirim_Army_Group "Yildirim Army Group")'s [Seventh Army](/wiki/Seventh_Army_(Ottoman_Empire) "Seventh Army (Ottoman Empire)") which held a defensive position in front of Nablus, and which the [Eighth Army](/wiki/Eighth_Army_(Ottoman_Empire) "Eighth Army (Ottoman Empire)") had attempted to retreat to, in vain.[[31]](#cite_note-33)

The [1927 Jericho earthquake](/wiki/1927_Jericho_earthquake "1927 Jericho earthquake") destroyed many of the Nablus' historic buildings, including the An-Nasr Mosque.[[32]](#cite_note-34) Though they were subsequently rebuilt by [Haj Amin al-Husayni](/wiki/Haj_Amin_al-Husayni "Haj Amin al-Husayni")'s [Supreme Muslim Council](/wiki/Supreme_Muslim_Council "Supreme Muslim Council") in the mid-1930s, their previous "picturesque" character was lost. During the [1936–1939 Arab revolt in Palestine](/wiki/1936%E2%80%931939_Arab_revolt_in_Palestine "1936–1939 Arab revolt in Palestine"), the British authorities demolished buildings in the Old City quarter of Qaryun suspected of harboring insurgents or hiding weapons.[[33]](#cite_note-Doumani6-35) [Jewish immigration](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah") did not significantly impact the demographic composition of Nablus, and it was slated for inclusion in the Arab state envisioned by the [United Nations General Assembly](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly")'s [1947 partition plan for Palestine](/wiki/United_Nations_Partition_Plan_for_Palestine "United Nations Partition Plan for Palestine").[[34]](#cite_note-36)

### Jordanian occupation

During the [1948 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War"), Nablus came under [Jordanian](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan") occupation. Thousands of [Palestinian refugees](/wiki/Palestinian_refugees "Palestinian refugees") displaced from areas captured by [Israeli](/wiki/Israel "Israel") forces arrived in Nablus, settling in refugee camps in and around the city. Its population doubled, and the influx of refugees put a heavy strain on the city's resources. Three such camps are still located within the city limits today: [Ein Beit al-Ma'](/wiki/Ein_Beit_al-Ma%27 "Ein Beit al-Ma'"), [Balata](/wiki/Balata_Camp "Balata Camp") and [Askar](/wiki/Askar_(camp) "Askar (camp)"). During the Jordanian occupation, the adjacent villages of [Rafidia](/wiki/Rafidia "Rafidia"), [Balata al-Balad](/wiki/Balata_al-Balad "Balata al-Balad"), al-Juneid and Askar were annexed to the Nablus municipality.[[35]](#cite_note-37) Nablus was [occupied and annexed by Jordan](/wiki/Jordanian_annexation_of_the_West_Bank "Jordanian annexation of the West Bank") in 1950.[[36]](#cite_note-38)

### Israeli period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/2018_OCHA_OpT_map_Nablus.jpg/250px-2018_OCHA_OpT_map_Nablus.jpg)](/wiki/File:2018_OCHA_OpT_map_Nablus.jpg)

2018 [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations_Office_for_the_Coordination_of_Humanitarian_Affairs "United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs") map of the area, showing the [Israeli occupation](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_West_Bank "Israeli occupation of the West Bank") arrangements.

The [Six-Day War](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War") ended with the [Israeli occupation](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_West_Bank "Israeli occupation of the West Bank") of Nablus. Many [Israeli settlements](/wiki/Israeli_settlement "Israeli settlement") were built around Nablus during the 1980s and early 1990s. The restrictions placed on Nablus during the [First Intifada](/wiki/First_Intifada "First Intifada") were met by a back-to-the-land movement to secure self-sufficiency, and had a notable outcome in boosting local agricultural production.[[37]](#cite_note-Robinson-39)

[Bassam Shakaa](/wiki/Bassam_Shakaa "Bassam Shakaa") was elected mayor in 1976, and four years later he survived an assassination attempt by the [Jewish Underground](/wiki/Jewish_Underground "Jewish Underground") on 2 June 1980, considered a terrorist group by Israel, which resulted in Shakaa losing both his legs. The Israeli administration removed him from office in the spring of 1982, and installed an army officer who ran the city for the following three and a half years.[[38]](#cite_note-Publishers_p._6-40)

In response to the murder of two Israeli teachers on 21 July near [Jenin](/wiki/Jenin "Jenin") and the killing of another Israeli on 30 July, the Israeli army imposed a 5-day curfew on the city on 29 July 1985 that was lifted 2 hours a day. [Najah University](/wiki/An-Najah_University "An-Najah University") was closed for two months for hanging PLO propaganda posters.[[39]](#cite_note-41)

The Israeli administration ended in January 1986 with the appointment of [Zafer al-Masri](/wiki/Zafer_al-Masri "Zafer al-Masri") as mayor. A popular leader of the Nablus Chamber of Commerce, al-Masri began a program of improvements in the town. Despite maintaining that he would have nothing to do with Israeli autonomy plans, he was assassinated on 2 March 1986, widely believed to be the work of the [Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine](/wiki/Popular_Front_for_the_Liberation_of_Palestine "Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine").[[38]](#cite_note-Publishers_p._6-40)

Salah el Bah'sh was shot with a [rubber bullet](/wiki/Rubber_bullet "Rubber bullet") by an Israeli soldier on 18 June 1989 whilst walking through the Nablus [Casbah](#Old_City). Witnesses told [B'Tselem](/wiki/B%27Tselem "B'Tselem") that he was shot in the chest at close range after not responding to a soldier shouting "Ta'amod" (Halt!). Bah'sh died and the army indicated that an investigation was being carried out.[[40]](#cite_note-42)

### Palestinian control

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Huwwara_Checkpoint_Palestine.jpg/250px-Huwwara_Checkpoint_Palestine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Huwwara_Checkpoint_Palestine.jpg)

View of [Huwwara checkpoint](/wiki/Huwwara_checkpoint "Huwwara checkpoint") with Palestinians waiting to travel south, 2006

Jurisdiction over the city was handed over to the [Palestinian National Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_National_Authority "Palestinian National Authority") on 12 December 1995, as a result of the [Oslo Accords](/wiki/Oslo_Accords "Oslo Accords") [Interim Agreement on the West Bank and the Gaza Strip](/wiki/Interim_Agreement_on_the_West_Bank_and_the_Gaza_Strip "Interim Agreement on the West Bank and the Gaza Strip").[[41]](#cite_note-43) Nablus is neighbored by [Israeli settlements](/wiki/Israeli_settlements "Israeli settlements"), and was site of regular clashes with the [Israel Defense Forces](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces") (IDF) during the First Intifada when the local prison was known for torture.[[42]](#cite_note-Stanley-44)
The city was a hub of [Palestinian nationalist](/wiki/Palestinian_nationalism "Palestinian nationalism") activity in the West Bank in the 1990s, and when the [Second Intifada](/wiki/Second_Intifada "Second Intifada") began, arsonists of Jewish shrines in Nablus were applauded.[[43]](#cite_note-Israeli-45) After the controversy over the [Muhammad cartoons in *Jyllands-Posten*](/wiki/Jyllands-Posten_Muhammad_cartoons_controversy "Jyllands-Posten Muhammad cartoons controversy"), published in Denmark in late September 2006, militias kidnapped two foreigners and threatened to kidnap more as a protest. Noa Meir, an Israeli military spokeswoman, said in 2008 that the city remains "capital of terror" of the West Bank.[[44]](#cite_note-46)

From the start of the [Second Intifada](/wiki/Second_Intifada "Second Intifada"), which began in September 2000, Nablus became a flash-point of clashes between the IDF and Palestinians. The city has a tradition of political activism, as evinced by its nickname, *jabal al-nar* (mountain of fire)[[37]](#cite_note-Robinson-39) and, located between two mountains, was closed off at both ends of the valley by Israeli checkpoints. For several years, movements in and out of the city were highly restricted.[[6]](#cite_note-Bishara-8) Nablus produced more [Palestinian suicide attacks](/wiki/Palestinian_suicide_attacks "Palestinian suicide attacks") than any other city during the Second Intifada.[[45]](#cite_note-47) The city and the [refugee camps](/wiki/Palestinian_refugee_camps "Palestinian refugee camps") of [Balata](/wiki/Balata_Camp "Balata Camp") and [Askar](/wiki/Askar_(camp) "Askar (camp)") constituted the center of "knowhow" for the production and operation of the rockets in the West Bank.[[46]](#cite_note-intelligence-48)

According to the [United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs](/wiki/United_Nations_Office_for_the_Coordination_of_Humanitarian_Affairs "United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs"), 522 residents of Nablus and surrounding refugee camps, including civilians, were killed and 3,104 injured during IDF military operations from 2000 to 2005.[[13]](#cite_note-OCHA-15) In April 2002, following the [Passover massacre](/wiki/Passover_massacre "Passover massacre")—an attack by Palestinian militants that killed 30 Israeli civilians attending a [seder](/wiki/Passover_Seder "Passover Seder") dinner at the Park Hotel in Netanya—Israel launched [Operation Defensive Shield](/wiki/Operation_Defensive_Shield "Operation Defensive Shield"), a major [military operation](/wiki/Battle_of_Nablus "Battle of Nablus") targeting in particular Nablus and Jenin. At least 80 Palestinians were killed in Nablus during the operation and several houses were destroyed or severely damaged.[[47]](#cite_note-AI-49)

The operation also resulted in severe damage to the historic core of the city, with 64 heritage buildings being heavily damaged or destroyed.[[42]](#cite_note-Stanley-44) IDF forces reentered Nablus during [Operation Determined Path](/wiki/Operation_Determined_Path "Operation Determined Path") in June 2002, remaining inside the city until the end of September. Over those three months, there had been more than 70 days of full 24-hour curfews.[[47]](#cite_note-AI-49) According to [Gush Shalom](/wiki/Gush_Shalom "Gush Shalom"), IDF bulldozers damaged the al-Khadra Mosque, the Great Mosque, the al-Satoon Mosque and the [Greek Orthodox Church](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Church "Greek Orthodox Church") in 2002. Some 60 houses were destroyed, and parts of the stone-paving in the old city were damaged. The al-Shifa *[hammam](/wiki/Turkish_bath "Turkish bath")* was hit by three rockets from [Apache helicopters](/wiki/Apache_helicopter "Apache helicopter"). The eastern entrance of the Khan al-Wikala (old market) and three soap factories were destroyed in [F-16](/wiki/F-16_Fighting_Falcon "F-16 Fighting Falcon") bombings. The cost of the damage was estimated at $80 million US.[[48]](#cite_note-50)

The Old City of Nablus became a site of [fierce clashes](/wiki/August_2016_Nablus_clashes "August 2016 Nablus clashes") in August 2016 between a militant group and [Palestinian police](/wiki/Palestinian_Civil_Police_Force "Palestinian Civil Police Force"). Two Palestinian Police officers were killed by terrorists in the city on 18 August.[[49]](#cite_note-albawaba-51) Shortly after, the police raid on the suspected areas in the Old City deteriorated into a gun battle, in which three armed militants were killed, including one killed by beating following his arrest.[[49]](#cite_note-albawaba-51) The person beaten to death was the suspected “mastermind” behind the August 18 shooting - Ahmed Izz Halaweh, a senior member of the armed wing of the Fatah movement the al-Aqsa Martyrs' Brigades.[[49]](#cite_note-albawaba-51) His death was branded by the UN and Palestinian factions as a part of “extrajudicial executions.”[[49]](#cite_note-albawaba-51) A widespread manhunt for multiple gunmen was initiated by the police as a result, concluding with the arrest of one suspect Salah al-Kurdi on 25 August.[[49]](#cite_note-albawaba-51)

## Geography

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/IsraelCVFRtopography.jpg/250px-IsraelCVFRtopography.jpg)](/wiki/File:IsraelCVFRtopography.jpg)

Section of topographical map of Nablus area

Nablus lies in a strategic position at a junction between two ancient commercial roads; one linking the [Sharon coastal plain](/wiki/Sharon_plain "Sharon plain") to the [Jordan valley](/wiki/Jordan_Valley_(Middle_East) "Jordan Valley (Middle East)"), the other linking Nablus to the [Galilee](/wiki/Galilee "Galilee") in the north, and the biblical [Judea](/wiki/Judea "Judea") to the south through the mountains.[[50]](#cite_note-AR-52) The city stands at an elevation of around 550 meters (1,800 ft) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level"),[[51]](#cite_note-NS-53) in a narrow valley running roughly east–west between two mountains: [Mount Ebal](/wiki/Mount_Ebal "Mount Ebal"), the northern mountain, is the taller peak at 940 meters (3,080 ft), while [Mount Gerizim](/wiki/Mount_Gerizim "Mount Gerizim"), the southern mountain, is 881 meters (2,890 ft) high.

Nablus is located 42 kilometers (26 mi) east of [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv"), [Israel](/wiki/State_of_Israel "State of Israel"), 110 kilometers (68 mi) west of [Amman](/wiki/Amman "Amman"), [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan") and 63 kilometers (39 mi) north of Jerusalem.[[51]](#cite_note-NS-53) Nearby cities and towns include [Huwara](/wiki/Huwara "Huwara") and [Aqraba](/wiki/Aqraba,_Nablus "Aqraba, Nablus") to the south, [Beit Furik](/wiki/Beit_Furik "Beit Furik") to the southeast, [Tammun](/wiki/Tammun "Tammun") to the northeast, [Asira ash-Shamaliya](/wiki/Asira_ash-Shamaliya "Asira ash-Shamaliya") to the north and [Kafr Qaddum](/wiki/Kafr_Qaddum "Kafr Qaddum") and [Tell](/wiki/Tell_(town) "Tell (town)") to the west.[[52]](#cite_note-54)

### Old City

Main article: [Old City of Nablus](/wiki/Old_City_of_Nablus "Old City of Nablus")

See also: [Levantine archaeology § Nablus](/wiki/Levantine_archaeology#Nablus "Levantine archaeology")

In the center of Nablus lies the old city, composed of six major quarters: Yasmina, Gharb, Qaryun, Aqaba, Qaysariyya, and Habala. Habala is the largest quarter and its population growth led to the development of two smaller neighborhoods: al-Arda and Tal al-Kreim. The old city is densely populated and prominent families include the Nimrs, Tuqans, and Abd al-Hadis. The large fortress-like compound of the [Abd al-Hadi Palace](/wiki/Abd_al-Hadi_Palace "Abd al-Hadi Palace") built in the 19th century is located in Qaryun. The [Nimr Hall](/wiki/Al-Nimr_Palace "Al-Nimr Palace") and the [Tuqan Palace](/wiki/Tuqan_Palace "Tuqan Palace") are located in the center of the old city. There are several [mosques](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque") in the Old City: the [Great Mosque of Nablus](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Nablus "Great Mosque of Nablus"), An-Nasr Mosque, al-Tina Mosque, [al-Khadra Mosque](/wiki/Al-Khadra_Mosque "Al-Khadra Mosque"), [Hanbali Mosque](/wiki/Hanbali_Mosque "Hanbali Mosque"), al-Anbia Mosque, Ajaj Mosque and others.[[53]](#cite_note-Semplici-a-55)
There are six *hamaams* ([Turkish baths](/wiki/Turkish_bath "Turkish bath")) in the Old City, the most prominent of them being al-Shifa and al-Hana. Al-Shifa was built by the Tuqans in 1624. Al-Hana in Yasmina was the last *hamaam* built in the city in the 19th century. It was closed in 1928 but restored and reopened in 1994.[[12]](#cite_note-Semplici-14) Several leather tanneries, *[souks](/wiki/Souk "Souk")*, pottery and textile workshops line the Old City streets.[[51]](#cite_note-NS-53)[[54]](#cite_note-Doumani2-56) Also located in the Old City is the 15th-century [Khan al-Tujjar](/wiki/Khan_al-Tujjar_(Nablus) "Khan al-Tujjar (Nablus)") caravanserai and the [Manara Clock Tower](/wiki/Manara_Clock_Tower "Manara Clock Tower"), built in 1906.[[51]](#cite_note-NS-53)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Nablus_-_eastern_panorama.jpg/960px-Nablus_-_eastern_panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nablus_-_eastern_panorama.jpg "Picture showing to the right the mountain \"Ebal\" with the rock of \"Sit Islamieh\", and to the left the south mountain \"Jirziem\" with an IDF military post on the far left")

### Climate

The relatively temperate [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") brings hot, dry summers and cool, rainy winters to Nablus. Spring arrives around March–April and the hottest months in Nablus are July and August with the average high being 29.6 °C (85.3 °F). The coldest month is January with temperatures usually at 6.2 °C (43.2 °F). Rain generally falls between October and March, with annual precipitation rates being approximately 656 mm (25.8 in).[[51]](#cite_note-NS-53)
