In 1596, the population consisted of 806 Muslim households, 20 [Samaritan](/wiki/Samaritan "Samaritan") households, 18 Christian households, and 15 Jewish households.[[26]](#cite_note-Hutteroth-28) Local Ottoman authorities recorded a population of around 20,000 residents in Nablus in 1849.[[57]](#cite_note-BD-59) In 1867 American visitors found the town to have a population of 4,000 'the chief part of whom are Mohammedans', with some Jews and Christians and 'about 150 Samaritans'.[[66]](#cite_note-68) In the [1922 British census of Palestine](/wiki/1922_census_of_Palestine "1922 census of Palestine"), there were a total of 15,947 inhabitants (15,238 Muslims, 544 Christians, 147 Samaritans, 16 Jews, and two Druze).[[59]](#cite_note-Census1922a-61) Population continued to grow, rising to 17,189 (16,483 Muslims, 533 Christians, 160 Samaritans, seven Druze, and six Jews) at the [1931 census of Palestine](/wiki/1931_census_of_Palestine "1931 census of Palestine") with 309 in nearby suburbs (225 Muslims and 84 Christians).[[60]](#cite_note-Census1931-62)

The 1938 village statistics show a further increase to 19,200.[[67]](#cite_note-69) The [1945 village statistics](/wiki/Village_Statistics,_1945 "Village Statistics, 1945") list the population as 23,250 (22,360 Muslims, 680 Christians, and 120 "other").[[68]](#cite_note-70)

According to the [Palestinian Central Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/Palestinian_Central_Bureau_of_Statistics "Palestinian Central Bureau of Statistics") (PCBS), Nablus had a population of 126,132 in 2007.[[65]](#cite_note-PCBS07-67) In the PCBS's 1997 census, the city had a population of 100,034, including 23,397 [refugees](/wiki/Palestinian_refugees "Palestinian refugees"), accounting for about 24% of the city's residents.[[69]](#cite_note-71) Nablus' Old City had a population of 12,000 in 2006.[[12]](#cite_note-Semplici-14) The population of Nablus city comprises 40% of its [governorate](/wiki/Nablus_Governorate "Nablus Governorate")'s inhabitants.[[65]](#cite_note-PCBS07-67)

Approximately half of population is under 20 years old. In 1997, the age distribution of the city's inhabitants was 28.4% under the age of 10, 20.8% from 10 to 19, 17.7% from 20 to 29, 18% from 30 to 44, 11.1% from 45 to 64 and 3.7% above the age of 65. The gender distribution was 50,945 males (50.92%) and 49,089 females (49.07%).[[70]](#cite_note-72)

### Religion

In 891 CE, during the early centuries of [Islamic](/wiki/Islam "Islam") rule, Nablus had a religiously diverse population of Samaritans, [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") and Christians. Arab geographer [Al-Dimashqi](/wiki/Shams_al-Din_al-Ansari_al-Dimashqi "Shams al-Din al-Ansari al-Dimashqi"), recorded that under the rule of the Mamluk Dynasty (Muslim Dynasty based in Egypt), local Muslims, Samaritans, Orthodox Christians, Catholics and Jews populated the city.[[22]](#cite_note-le_Strange-24) At the [1931 census](/wiki/1931_census_of_Palestine "1931 census of Palestine"), the population was counted as 16,483 Muslims, 533 Christians, 6 Jews, 7 Druses and 160 Samaritans.[[60]](#cite_note-Census1931-62) However, this census was taken after the [1929 Palestine riots](/wiki/1929_Palestine_riots "1929 Palestine riots") which drove the Jews out of many majority-Arab cities.[[71]](#cite_note-73)

The majority of the inhabitants today are Muslim, but there are small [Christian](/wiki/Palestinian_Christian "Palestinian Christian") and [Samaritan](/wiki/Samaritan "Samaritan") communities as well. Much of the local [Palestinian](/wiki/Palestinian_people "Palestinian people") Muslim population of Nablus is believed to be descended from Samaritans who converted to Islam. Certain Nabulsi family names are associated with Samaritan ancestry – Muslimani, Yaish, and Shakshir among others.[[72]](#cite_note-74) According to the historian Fayyad Altif, large numbers of Samaritans converted because of persecution and because the monotheistic nature of Islam made it easy for them to accept it.[[73]](#cite_note-Ireton-75)

In 1967, there were about 3,500 Christians of various denominations in Nablus, but that figure dwindled to about 650 in 2008.[[74]](#cite_note-76) Of the Christian populace, there are seventy [Orthodox Christian](/wiki/Orthodoxy#Christianity "Orthodoxy") families, about thirty Catholic (Roman Catholic and Eastern Melkite Catholic) families and thirty [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") families. Most Christians used to live in the suburb of [Rafidia](/wiki/Rafidia "Rafidia") in the western part of the city.[[12]](#cite_note-Semplici-14)

There are seventeen Islamic monuments and eleven mosques in the Old City.[[13]](#cite_note-OCHA-15)[[75]](#cite_note-NW-77) Nine of the mosques were established before the 15th century.[[13]](#cite_note-OCHA-15) In addition to Muslim houses of worship, Nablus contains an Orthodox church dedicated to Saint Justin Martyr,[[12]](#cite_note-Semplici-14) built in 1898, and the ancient Samaritan synagogue, which is still in use.[[75]](#cite_note-NW-77)

## Economy

Beginning in the early 16th century, trade networks connecting Nablus to [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus") and [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo") were supplemented by the establishment of trading posts in the [Hejaz](/wiki/Hejaz "Hejaz") and [Gulf](/wiki/Arab_states_of_the_Persian_Gulf "Arab states of the Persian Gulf") regions to the south and east, as well as in the [Anatolian Peninsula](/wiki/Anatolian_Peninsula "Anatolian Peninsula") and the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean") islands of [Crete](/wiki/Crete "Crete") and [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus"). Nablus also developed trade relations with [Aleppo](/wiki/Aleppo "Aleppo"), [Mosul](/wiki/Mosul "Mosul"), and [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad").[[54]](#cite_note-Doumani2-56) The Ottoman government oversaw the safety and funding of the annual [hajj](/wiki/Hajj "Hajj") (*qafilat al-hajj*) from [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus") to the Islamic holy cities of [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca") and [Medina](/wiki/Medina "Medina"). Pilgrimage caravans became a key factor in the fiscal and political relationship between Nablus and the central government. For a brief period in the early 17th century, the governor of Nablus, [Farrukh Pasha](/wiki/Farrukh_Pasha "Farrukh Pasha"), was appointed leader of
the caravan (*[amir al-hajj](/wiki/Amir_al-hajj "Amir al-hajj")*), and he constructed a large commercial compound in Nablus for that purpose.[[54]](#cite_note-Doumani2-56)

In 1882, there were 32 soap factories and 400 [looms](/wiki/Loom "Loom") exporting their products throughout the Middle East.[[12]](#cite_note-Semplici-14)[[76]](#cite_note-IO-78) Nablus exported three-fourths of its soap — the city's most important commodity—to Cairo by caravan through [Gaza](/wiki/Gaza_City "Gaza City") and the [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula"), and by sea through the ports of [Jaffa](/wiki/Jaffa "Jaffa") and Gaza. From Egypt, and particularly from Cairo and [Damietta](/wiki/Damietta "Damietta"), Nablus merchants imported mainly rice, [sugar](/wiki/Sugar "Sugar"), and spices, as well as linen, cotton, and wool textiles. Cotton, soap, olive oil, and textiles were exported to Damascus, whence silks, high-quality textiles, copper, and a number luxury items, such as jewellery were imported.[[54]](#cite_note-Doumani2-56) Outside the city limits, extensive fields of [olive](/wiki/Olive "Olive") groves, [fig](/wiki/Ficus "Ficus") and [pomegranate](/wiki/Pomegranate "Pomegranate") orchards and [grape](/wiki/Grape "Grape") vineyards covered the slopes. Crops such as tomatoes, cucumbers, melons and *[mulukhiyya](/wiki/Mulukhiyya "Mulukhiyya")* were grown in the fields and grain mills were scattered across central [Samaria](/wiki/Samaria "Samaria").[[54]](#cite_note-Doumani2-56) Nablus was also the largest producer of [cotton](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton") in the Levant, producing over 225,000 kg (496,040 lb) of the product by 1837.[[30]](#cite_note-Doumani5-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Placa_dels_m%C3%A0rtirs_Nablus.jpg/250px-Placa_dels_m%C3%A0rtirs_Nablus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Placa_dels_m%C3%A0rtirs_Nablus.jpg)

Downtown Nablus, Martyrs Square

Today, Nablus has a bustling modern commercial center with restaurants, and [shopping malls](/wiki/Shopping_mall "Shopping mall").[[77]](#cite_note-Assadi-79)[[78]](#cite_note-:2-80) Traditional industries
[[51]](#cite_note-NS-53) such as the production of soap, olive oil, and [Palestinian handicrafts](/wiki/Palestinian_handicrafts "Palestinian handicrafts") continue to operate in Nablus.
[[78]](#cite_note-:2-80) Other industries include furniture production, tile production, stone quarrying, textile manufacturing and [leather tanning](/wiki/Leather_tanning "Leather tanning").[[78]](#cite_note-:2-80) The city is widely known for sweets like kunafah, olive oil, soap and ice-cream.[[78]](#cite_note-:2-80)

The Vegetable Oil Industry Co. is a Nablus factory that produces olive oil, and vegetable butter which is exported to [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan").[[51]](#cite_note-NS-53) In 2000, the al-Huda Textiles factory in Nablus
produced 500 pieces of clothing daily; however, production dropped to 150–200 pieces in 2002. Al-Huda imports textiles from [China](/wiki/China "China") and exports finished products to [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel").[[13]](#cite_note-OCHA-15) In 2003, there were eight restaurants and four hotels — the largest being al-Qasr and al-Yasmeen.[[79]](#cite_note-KimLeep354-81) The soap industry has suffered from the [West Bank closures](/wiki/West_Bank_closures "West Bank closures") and IDF incursions. In 2008, only two soap factories were still open.[[80]](#cite_note-82)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/%D8%B3%D9%88%D9%82_%D9%81%D9%8A_%D9%86%D8%A7%D8%A8%D9%84%D8%B3.jpg/250px-%D8%B3%D9%88%D9%82_%D9%81%D9%8A_%D9%86%D8%A7%D8%A8%D9%84%D8%B3.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D8%B3%D9%88%D9%82_%D9%81%D9%8A_%D9%86%D8%A7%D8%A8%D9%84%D8%B3.jpg)

One of the old markets in Nablus

The Al-Arz ice-cream company is the largest of six ice-cream manufacturers in the Palestinian territories. The Nablus business developed from an ice-factory set up by Mohammad Anabtawi in the town centre in 1950. It produces 50 tons a day, and exports to Jordan and Iraq. Most of the ingredients are imported from Israel.[[81]](#cite_note-LevyHaaretz-83)

Before 2000, 13.4% of Nablus' residents worked in Israel, with the figure dropping to 4.7% in 2004. The city's manufacturing sector made up 15.7% of the economy in 2004, a drop from 21% in 2000. Since 2000, most of the workforce has been employed in agriculture and local trade.[[13]](#cite_note-OCHA-15) In the wake of the Intifada, unemployment rates rose from 14.2% in 1997 to 60% in 2004. According to an [OCHA](/wiki/OCHA "OCHA") report in 2008, one of the reasons for the high unemployment was a ring of checkpoints around the city,[[82]](#cite_note-ocha1-84) leading to the relocation of many businesses.[[83]](#cite_note-85)

Since the removal of the [Hawara](/wiki/Huwara "Huwara") roadblock, the casbah has become a vibrant marketplace.[[81]](#cite_note-LevyHaaretz-83) Nablus is home to the [Palestine Securities Exchange](/wiki/Palestine_Securities_Exchange "Palestine Securities Exchange") (PSE) and the al-Quds Financial Index, housed in the al-Qasr building in the Rafidia suburb of the city. The PSE's first trading session took place on 19 February 1997. In 2007, the capitalization of the PSE topped 3.5 million [Jordanian dinars](/wiki/Jordanian_dinar "Jordanian dinar").[[12]](#cite_note-Semplici-14)

## Education

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/An-Najah_University%2C_Nablus_002_-_Aug_2011.jpg/250px-An-Najah_University%2C_Nablus_002_-_Aug_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:An-Najah_University,_Nablus_002_-_Aug_2011.jpg)

[An-Najah University](/wiki/An-Najah_University "An-Najah University"), Nablus

According to the [Palestinian Central Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/Palestinian_Central_Bureau_of_Statistics "Palestinian Central Bureau of Statistics") (PCBS), in 1997, 44,926 were enrolled in schools (41.2% in primary school, 36.2% in secondary school, and 22.6% in high school). About 19.8% of high school students received bachelor diplomas or higher diplomas.[[84]](#cite_note-86) In 2006, there were 234 schools and 93,925 students in the [Nablus Governorate](/wiki/Nablus_Governorate "Nablus Governorate"); 196 schools are run by the [Education Ministry of the Palestinian National Authority](/wiki/Education_Minister_of_the_Palestinian_National_Authority "Education Minister of the Palestinian National Authority"), 14 by the [United Nations Relief and Works Agency](/wiki/United_Nations_Relief_and_Works_Agency_for_Palestine_Refugees_in_the_Near_East "United Nations Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees in the Near East") (UNRWA) and 24 are private schools.[[85]](#cite_note-87)

Nablus is also home to [an-Najah National University](/wiki/An-Najah_National_University "An-Najah National University"), the largest [Palestinian university](/wiki/List_of_Palestinian_universities "List of Palestinian universities") in the West Bank.[[86]](#cite_note-:0-88) Founded in 1918 by the an-Najah Nabulsi School, it became a college in 1941 and a university in 1977.[[86]](#cite_note-:0-88) An-Najah was closed down by Israeli authorities during the First Intifada, but reopened in 1991.[[86]](#cite_note-:0-88) Today, the university has three campuses in Nablus with over 16,500 students and 300 professors.[[86]](#cite_note-:0-88) The university's faculties include seven in the [humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities") and nine in the [sciences](/wiki/Sciences "Sciences").[[86]](#cite_note-:0-88)

Nablus has been ranked as the 5th best city in the Middle East to learn Arabic.[[87]](#cite_note-:1-89) For non-Arabic aspirants, An-Najah University has faculties, providing courses related to Arabic language.[[87]](#cite_note-:1-89)

## Healthcare

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Specialized_Arab_Hospital%2C_Nablus_001.jpg/250px-Specialized_Arab_Hospital%2C_Nablus_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Specialized_Arab_Hospital,_Nablus_001.jpg)

Nablus Speciality Hospital in 2019

There are six [hospitals](/wiki/Hospital "Hospital") in Nablus, the four major ones being al-Ittihad, St. Lukes, al-Watani (the National), and the [Rafidia Surgical Hospital](/wiki/Rafidia_Surgical_Hospital "Rafidia Surgical Hospital"), which is located in the western neighborhood of [Rafidiya](/wiki/Rafidiya "Rafidiya"), and is the largest hospital in the city. Al-Watani Hospital specializes in [oncology](/wiki/Oncology "Oncology") services.[[13]](#cite_note-OCHA-15) The [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") St. Lukes hospital was founded in 1900 by the medical missionary [Gaskoin Wright](/wiki/Gaskoin_Richard_Morden_Wright "Gaskoin Richard Morden Wright"); the National Hospital was founded in 1910.[[51]](#cite_note-NS-53)[[88]](#cite_note-NMG-90)[[89]](#cite_note-91) In addition to hospitals, Nablus contains the al-Rahma and at-Tadamon clinics, the al-Razi medical center, the Amal Center for Rehabilitation and 68 pharmacies.[[88]](#cite_note-NMG-90) The Nablus Speciality Hospital was built in 2001, which is specializes in [open heart surgery](/wiki/Open_heart_surgery "Open heart surgery"), [angiograms](/wiki/Angiogram "Angiogram") and [angioplasty](/wiki/Angioplasty "Angioplasty").

## Culture and arts

Nablus and its culture enjoy a certain renown throughout the Palestinian Territories and the [Arab world](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world") with significant and unique contributions to [Palestinian culture](/wiki/Palestinian_culture "Palestinian culture"), [cuisine](/wiki/Palestinian_cuisine "Palestinian cuisine") and [costume](/wiki/Palestinian_costumes "Palestinian costumes"). *Nabulsi*, meaning "from Nablus", is used to describe items such as [handicrafts](/wiki/Palestinian_handicrafts "Palestinian handicrafts") (e.g. [Nabulsi soap](/wiki/Nabulsi_soap "Nabulsi soap")) and food products (e.g. [Nabulsi cheese](/wiki/Nabulsi_cheese "Nabulsi cheese")) that are made in Nablus or in the traditional Nablus style.

### Customs

*Al-Shaʿbūniyya* (الشعبونية) is a long-standing Nabulsi social custom associated with the middle of [Sha'ban](/wiki/Sha%27ban "Sha'ban"), centered on maintaining kinship ties, particularly among women. Families traditionally exchange home-prepared foods and sweets, and women make visits to relatives and neighbors as an expression of maintaining family bonds ([صلة الرحم](https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D8%B5%D9%84%D8%A9_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%B1%D8%AD%D9%85 "ar:صلة الرحم")). Contemporary accounts note changes in scale and form due to urban life, economic pressures, and disruptions such as the COVID-19 pandemic.[[90]](#cite_note-newArabApr2019-92)[[91]](#cite_note-AJMay2018-93)[[92]](#cite_note-94)[[93]](#cite_note-95)

### Traditional costume

Main article: [Palestinian costumes](/wiki/Palestinian_costumes "Palestinian costumes")

Nablus costume was of a distinctive style that employed colorful combinations of various fabrics. Because of its position as important trade center with a flourishing *[souk](/wiki/Souk "Souk")* ("market"), in the late 19th century, there was a large choice of fabrics available in the city, from [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus") and [Aleppo](/wiki/Aleppo "Aleppo") [silk](/wiki/Silk "Silk") to [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester") [cottons](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton") and [calicos](/wiki/Calico_(fabric) "Calico (fabric)"). Similar in construction to the garments worn in the [Galilee](/wiki/Galilee "Galilee"), both long and short [Turkish style](/wiki/Turkish_culture "Turkish culture") jackets were worn over the *thob* ("robe"). For daily wear, *thobs* were often made of white cotton or [linen](/wiki/Linen "Linen"), with a preference for winged sleeves. In the summer, costumes often incorporated interwoven striped bands of red, green and yellow on the front and back, with appliqué and braidwork popularly decorating the *qabbeh* ("square chest piece").[[94]](#cite_note-PCA2-96)

### Cuisine

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Nablus_souq_kanafeh_131_-_Aug_2011.jpg/250px-Nablus_souq_kanafeh_131_-_Aug_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nablus_souq_kanafeh_131_-_Aug_2011.jpg)

A *siniyyeh* of [Kanafeh](/wiki/Kanafeh "Kanafeh") in a souq in Nablus

Nablus is one of the Palestinian cities that sustained elite classes, fostering the development of a culture of "high cuisine", such as that of [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus") or [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad"). The city is home to a number of food products well known throughout the Levant, the [Arab world](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world") and the former [provinces of the Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Provinces_of_the_Ottoman_Empire "Provinces of the Ottoman Empire").

*[Kanafeh](/wiki/Kanafeh "Kanafeh")* (or Kunafa) is the best known *Nabulsi* sweet.[[51]](#cite_note-NS-53) It is made of several fine shreds of pastry noodles with honey-sweetened cheese in the center. The top layer of the pastry is usually dyed orange with food coloring and sprinkled with crushed pistachios. Now made throughout the Middle East, *kanafeh Nabulsi* uses a white-brine cheese called *[jibneh Nabulsi](/wiki/Nabulsi_cheese "Nabulsi cheese")*. Boiled sugar is used as a syrup for *kanafeh*.

Other sweets made in Nablus include *[baklawa](/wiki/Baklawa "Baklawa")*, *[tamriyeh](/wiki/Tamriyeh "Tamriyeh")*, *mabrumeh* and *ghuraybeh*,[[95]](#cite_note-97) a plain pastry made of butter, flour and sugar in an "S"-shape, or shaped as fingers or bracelets.[[96]](#cite_note-98)

### Cultural centers

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Dabkeh.png/250px-Dabkeh.png)](/wiki/File:Dabkeh.png)

[Dabke](/wiki/Dabke "Dabke") dance group on [Mount Gerizim](/wiki/Mount_Gerizim "Mount Gerizim")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Old_city_of_Nablus.JPG/250px-Old_city_of_Nablus.JPG)](/wiki/File:Old_city_of_Nablus.JPG)

Alley in the Old City leading to and from the [souk](/wiki/Souk "Souk"), 2008

There are three cultural centers in Nablus. The Child Cultural Center (CCC), founded in 1998 and built in a renovated historic building, operates an art and drawing workshop, a stage for play performances, a music room, a children's library and a multimedia lab.[[97]](#cite_note-99) The Children Happiness Center (CHC) was also established in 1998. Its main activities include promoting Palestinian culture through social events, *[dabke](/wiki/Dabke "Dabke")* classes and field trips. In addition to national culture, the CHC has a [football](/wiki/Football_(soccer) "Football (soccer)") and [chess](/wiki/Chess "Chess") team.[[98]](#cite_note-100) The Nablus municipal government established its own cultural center in 2003, called the Nablus Municipality Cultural Center (NMCC) aimed at establishing and developing educational facilities.[[99]](#cite_note-101)

### Soap production

Main article: [Nabulsi soap](/wiki/Nabulsi_soap "Nabulsi soap")

Nabulsi soap or *sabon nabulsi* is a type of [castile soap](/wiki/Castile_soap "Castile soap") produced only in Nablus[[100]](#cite_note-Piefza-102) and made of three primary ingredients: virgin [olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil"), water, and a sodium[[101]](#cite_note-103) compound.[[102]](#cite_note-IMEU-104) Since the 10th century, Nabulsi soap has enjoyed a reputation for being a fine product,[[103]](#cite_note-SEMP-105) and has been exported across the Arab world and to Europe.[[102]](#cite_note-IMEU-104) Though the number of soap factories decreased from a peak of thirty in the 19th century to only two today, efforts to preserve this important part of Palestinian and Nabulsi cultural heritage continue.[[102]](#cite_note-IMEU-104)[[103]](#cite_note-SEMP-105)

Made in a cube-like shape about 1.5 inches (3.8 cm) tall and 2.25 by 2.25 inches (5.7 by 5.7 cm) wide, the color of Nabulsi soap is like that of "the page of an old book."[[103]](#cite_note-SEMP-105) The cubes are stamped on the top with the seal of the factory that produces it.[[104]](#cite_note-Shakaa-106) The soap's sodium compound came from the [barilla](/wiki/Barilla "Barilla") plant. Prior to the 1860s, in the summertime, the barilla would be placed in towering stacks, burned, and then the ashes and coals would be gathered into sacks, and transported to Nablus from the area of modern-day [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan") in large [caravans](/wiki/Camel_train "Camel train"). In the city, the ashes and coals were pounded into a fine natural [alkaline](/wiki/Alkaline "Alkaline") soda powder called *qilw*.[[103]](#cite_note-SEMP-105) Today, *qilw* is still used in combination with lime.

## Local government

The city of Nablus is the *muhfaza* (seat) of the [Nablus Governorate](/wiki/Nablus_Governorate "Nablus Governorate"), and is governed by a municipal council made up of fifteen elected members, including the mayor.[[105]](#cite_note-NMMC-107)

The two primary political parties in the municipal council are [Hamas](/wiki/Hamas "Hamas") and [Fatah](/wiki/Fatah "Fatah"). In the 2005 Palestinian municipal elections, the Reform and Change list representing the Hamas faction won 73.4% of the vote, gaining the majority of the municipal seats (13). Palestine Tomorrow, representing Fatah, gained the remaining two seats with 13.0% of the vote. Other political parties, such as the [Palestinian People's Party](/wiki/Palestinian_People%27s_Party "Palestinian People's Party") and the [Democratic Front for the Liberation of Palestine](/wiki/Democratic_Front_for_the_Liberation_of_Palestine "Democratic Front for the Liberation of Palestine") failed to gain any seats in the council, though they each received over 1,000 votes.[[106]](#cite_note-Results-108)

Yaish's four-year term legally expired in December 2009. While elections in the West Bank were scheduled for 17 July 2010, they were canceled because of Fatah's lack of agreement on list of candidates. Nablus was one of the most important municipalities where Fatah failed to resolve internal conflicts that resulted in two competing Fatah lists: one headed by former mayor [Ghassan Shakaa](/wiki/Ghassan_Shakaa "Ghassan Shakaa") and one headed by Amin Makboul.[[107]](#cite_note-109)

In the October 2012 municipal elections, Hamas boycotted the polls, protesting the holding of elections while reconciliation efforts with Fatah were at a standstill. Former mayor Ghassan Shakaa, a former local Fatah leader, won the vote as an independent against Fatah member Amin Makboul and another independent candidate.[[108]](#cite_note-110)[[109]](#cite_note-111)

### Mayors

Main article: [List of mayors of Nablus](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Nablus "List of mayors of Nablus")

Modern mayorship in Nablus began in 1869 with the appointment of Sheikh Mohammad Tuffaha by the Ottoman governor of Syria/Palestine. On 2 July 1980, [Bassam Shakaa](/wiki/Bassam_Shakaa "Bassam Shakaa"), then mayor of Nablus, lost both of his legs as a result of a [car bombing](/wiki/Car_bombing "Car bombing") carried out by Israeli militants affiliated with the [Gush Emunim Underground](/wiki/Gush_Emunim_Underground "Gush Emunim Underground") movement.[[110]](#cite_note-haaretz-112)

The current mayor, [Adly Yaish](/wiki/Adly_Yaish "Adly Yaish"), a Hamas member, was arrested by the Israel Defense Forces in May 2007, during [Operation Summer Rains](/wiki/Operation_Summer_Rains "Operation Summer Rains"), launched in retaliation for the kidnapping of Israeli soldier [Gilad Shalit](/wiki/Gilad_Shalit "Gilad Shalit") by Hamas.[[111]](#cite_note-ynet-113) Municipal council members Abdel Jabbar Adel Musa "Dweikat", Majida Fadda, Khulood El-Masri, and Mahdi Hanbali were also arrested.[[105]](#cite_note-NMMC-107) He spent 15 months in prison without being charged.[[112]](#cite_note-114)

## Municipal services

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Nablus_street.JPG/250px-Nablus_street.JPG)](/wiki/File:Nablus_street.JPG)

A street in Nablus leading to the Old City. [Minaret](/wiki/Minaret "Minaret") of An-Nasr Mosque in the background

In 1997, 99.7% of Nablus' 18,003 households were connected to electricity through a public network. Prior to its establishment in 1957, electricity came from private generators. Today, the majority of the inhabitants of 18 nearby towns, in addition to the city's inhabitants, are connected to the Nablus network.[[113]](#cite_note-115)

The majority of households are connected to a public sewage system (93%), with the remaining 7% connected through [cesspits](/wiki/Cesspit "Cesspit").[[114]](#cite_note-PCBS4-116) The sewage system, established n the early 1950s, also connects the refugee camps of Balata, Askar and Ein Beit al-Ma'.[[115]](#cite_note-WWWD-117) Pipe water is provided for 100% of the city's households, primarily through a public network (99.3%), but some residents receive water through a private system (0.7%).[[114]](#cite_note-PCBS4-116) The water network was established in 1932 by the British authorities and is fed by water from four nearby wells: [Deir Sharaf](/wiki/Deir_Sharaf "Deir Sharaf"), [Far'a](/wiki/Far%27a "Far'a"), [al-Badan](/wiki/Al-Badan "Al-Badan") and [Audala](/wiki/Audala "Audala").[[115]](#cite_note-WWWD-117)

### Fire department

Nablus is one of the few cities in the West Bank to have a fire department, which was founded in 1958. At that time, the "fire brigade" (as it was called) was composed of five members and one extinguishing vehicle. In 2007, the department had seventy members and over twenty vehicles. Until 1986, it was responsible for all of the northern West Bank, but today it only covers the Nablus and [Tubas Governorates](/wiki/Tubas_Governorate "Tubas Governorate"). From 1997 to 2006, Nablus' fire department extinguished 15,346 fires.[[116]](#cite_note-118)

## Transportation

In the early 20th century, Nablus was the southernmost station of a spur from the [Jezreel Valley railway](/wiki/Jezreel_Valley_railway "Jezreel Valley railway")'s [Afula](/wiki/Afula "Afula") station, itself a spur from the [Hejaz railway](/wiki/Hejaz_railway "Hejaz railway"). The extension of the railway to Nablus was built in 1911–12.[[117]](#cite_note-119) During the beginning of the British Mandate, one weekly train was operated from Haifa to Nablus via Afula and [Jenin](/wiki/Jenin "Jenin"). The railway was destroyed during the [1948 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War"), and the route of the line was bisected by the [Green Line](/wiki/Green_Line_(Israel) "Green Line (Israel)").

The main [Beersheba–Nazareth road](/wiki/Highway_60_(Israel%E2%80%93Palestine) "Highway 60 (Israel–Palestine)") running through the middle of the West Bank ends in Nablus, although the thoroughfare of local Arabs is severely restricted. The city was connected to [Tulkarm](/wiki/Tulkarm "Tulkarm"), [Qalqilya](/wiki/Qalqilya "Qalqilya") and [Jenin](/wiki/Jenin "Jenin") by roads which are now blocked by the [Israeli West Bank barrier](/wiki/Israeli_West_Bank_barrier "Israeli West Bank barrier"). From 2000 until 2011, Israel maintained [checkpoints](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces_checkpoint "Israel Defense Forces checkpoint") such as [Huwwara checkpoint](/wiki/Huwwara_checkpoint "Huwwara checkpoint") which effectively cut off the city, severely curtailing social and economic travel.[[118]](#cite_note-120) From January 2002, buses, taxis, trucks and private citizens required a permit from the Israeli military authorities to leave and enter Nablus.[[13]](#cite_note-OCHA-15) Since 2011, there has been a relaxation of travel restrictions and the dismantlement of some checkpoints.[[119]](#cite_note-121)

The nearest airport is the [Ben Gurion International Airport](/wiki/Ben_Gurion_International_Airport "Ben Gurion International Airport") in [Lod](/wiki/Lod "Lod"), [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), but because of restrictions governing the entry of [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians") to Israel, and their lack of access to foreign Embassies to get travel visas, many residents must travel to [Amman](/wiki/Amman "Amman"), [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan") to use the [Queen Alia International Airport](/wiki/Queen_Alia_International_Airport "Queen Alia International Airport"), which requires passage through a number of checkpoints and the Jordanian border. Taxis are the main form of public transportation within Nablus and the city contains 28 taxi offices and garages.[[120]](#cite_note-122)

## Sports

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Stadium8351.JPG/250px-Stadium8351.JPG)](/wiki/File:Stadium8351.JPG)

Nablus municipal stadium and surroundings

The Nablus [football](/wiki/Football_(soccer) "Football (soccer)") stadium has a capacity of 8,000.[[121]](#cite_note-123) The stadium is home to the city's football club [al-Ittihad](/wiki/Al-Ittihad_(Nablus) "Al-Ittihad (Nablus)"), which is in the main league of the Palestinian Territories.[[122]](#cite_note-124) The club participated in the Middle East Mediterranean Scholar Athlete Games in 2000.[[123]](#cite_note-125)
